questions.prompt: Cleanup effects should be applied in what order?

choices.text: DeHum → High-pass filter → Noise Reduction → De-click → EQ → DeEss → Compression → Limiter → Loudness normalization. Order matters because each effect either prepares the signal for the next or amplifies artifacts the previous steps left behind. 1) DeHum first removes electrical buzz at specific frequencies before broadband NR would amplify it. 2) High-pass cuts rumble/wind below ~80 Hz — cleaner input to subsequent processors. 3) Noise Reduction (broadband) removes hiss/chatter. 4) De-click removes transient artifacts. 5) EQ shapes frequency response on cleaner signal (EQ-ing a noisy signal carves up the noise). 6) DeEss tames sibilance (which EQ may have emphasized if presence-boosted). 7) Compression evens dynamics ONLY AFTER cleanup, otherwise compression amplifies the quiet hiss/clicks that should've been removed (classic novice mistake: compress first → background noise becomes loud → reduce noise → audio sounds artificial). 8) Limiter catches peaks. 9) Loudness normalization to target (-14 LUFS YouTube). The cleanup-before-dynamics principle is universal in pro audio engineering. In Premiere: apply effects in this order via Essential Sound (which orders them correctly internally) OR via the effect controls panel (manual ordering). In Audition: drag effect blocks in the effects rack to enforce order. Skipping the order produces 'cleaned but artifacted' audio that sounds worse than untouched.

questions.explanation: Cleanup before dynamics: DeHum → HP → NR → De-click → EQ → DeEss → Comp → Limiter → LUFS. Cleanup before compression; compression amplifies noise.

questions.prompt: Audio has obvious plosive ('P pops') at three spots in a 5-minute clip. What's the fastest pro fix?

choices.text: Three quick fixes per plosive: 1) Zoom in to the waveform spike at the plosive moment. 2) Apply a brief high-pass filter (cut at 80-100 Hz) for just that few frames — removes the low-frequency thump while preserving the consonant's higher-frequency content. Or: 3) Volume keyframe at the spike — drop volume 6-12 dB just at the plosive moment (5-10 frames), then return to normal level. The volume-keyframe method is fastest: select clip → audio rubber band → click to add keyframe just before the spike at normal level → click at the spike's loudest moment and drag down 6-12 dB → click just after the spike at normal level. Three plosives = ~6 keyframes total = 2 minutes of work. Alternative for surgical work: send clip to Audition; use Spectral Edit (Spectral Frequency Display); paint a low-frequency region at the plosive moment to remove just that energy while preserving everything else. Spectral is more precise but slower. For 3 plosives, keyframes win on speed; for 30 plosives in heavy editing, spectral work in Audition is investment-worthy. Prevention is even better: pop filter, mic angled 3-5° off-axis, voice training. But once it's recorded, plosive fix is mandatory — plosives in a finished mix sound amateurish and pop on small speakers (especially phones).

questions.explanation: Volume keyframes at each plosive (drop 6-12 dB for ~10 frames). Or high-pass at 80-100 Hz briefly. Audition Spectral Edit for surgical cases.
